clusters.title: Scientists map the brain’s hidden wiring using RNA barcodes in major breakthrough
clusters.summary: Researchers have developed a cutting-edge technique that uses RNA “barcodes” to map how neurons connect, capturing thousands of links with single-synapse precision. The method transforms brain mapping into a sequencing task, making it faster and more scalable than traditional approaches. In mice, it revealed surprising new connections between brain cells that were previously unknown.

items.title: Scientists map the brain’s hidden wiring using RNA barcodes in major breakthrough
items.description: Researchers have developed a cutting-edge technique that uses RNA “barcodes” to map how neurons connect, capturing thousands of links with single-synapse precision. The method transforms brain mapping into a sequencing task, making it faster and more scalable than traditional approaches. In mice, it revealed surprising new connections between brain cells that were previously unknown. This could open the door to earlier detection and targeted treatment of neurological diseases.

clusters.title: The human body isn't a masterpiece of design. It's a patchwork of evolutionary compromise
clusters.summary: The human body is often described as a marvel of "perfect design": elegant, efficient and finely tuned for its purpose. Yet, when we look closer, a rather different picture emerges.

items.title: The human body isn't a masterpiece of design. It's a patchwork of evolutionary compromise
items.description: The human body is often described as a marvel of "perfect design": elegant, efficient and finely tuned for its purpose. Yet, when we look closer, a rather different picture emerges.

clusters.title: New study shows linens, not equipment emissions, dominate ultrasound's carbon footprint
clusters.summary: A new research study published in the Journal of the American College of Radiology (JACR) has found that unlike other modalities, linens and disposable supplies account for the vast majority of ultrasound's annual carbon emissions—not equipment energy use. The study, titled "Harmonizing Diagnostic Ultrasound Practice with Environmental Sustainability: A Life Cycle Assessment of Diagnostic Ultrasound in a Single Adult University Hospital," determined that linens (35%) and other single-use supplies (34%) accounted for most of ultrasound's greenhouse gas emissions, whereas production (7%) and energy use (3%) of the ultrasound equipment were minor contributors.

items.title: New study shows linens, not equipment emissions, dominate ultrasound's carbon footprint

The study, titled "Harmonizing Diagnostic Ultrasound Practice with Environmental Sustainability: A Life Cycle Assessment of Diagnostic Ultrasound in a Single Adult University Hospital," determined that linens (35%) and other single-use supplies (34%) accounted for most of ultrasound's greenhouse gas emissions, whereas production (7%) and energy use (3%) of the ultrasound equipment were minor contributors.

clusters.title: This tiny implant, smaller than a grain of salt, can read your brain
clusters.summary: A new neural implant is so small it can rest on a grain of salt, yet it can track and wirelessly transmit brain activity for over a year. It’s powered by laser light that safely passes through tissue and communicates using tiny infrared signals. This ultra-miniature device could transform how scientists study the brain without invasive wiring.

items.title: This tiny implant, smaller than a grain of salt, can read your brain

It’s powered by laser light that safely passes through tissue and communicates using tiny infrared signals. This ultra-miniature device could transform how scientists study the brain without invasive wiring.

clusters.title: Teen and young adult cancer survivors face double the risk of later cancers, finds study
clusters.summary: Survivors of cancer in their teen and young adult years are at double the risk of most types of later cancers, according to research from Alberta published in the Canadian Medical Association Journal. Primary neoplasms—new cancers that develop after an earlier cancer—are a known late effect of previous cancers, often because of the effects of treatment.

clusters.title: Will Insurance Cover Ozempic for Sleep Apnea?
clusters.summary: Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1s) like Ozempic can help with weight loss, which may assist with sleep apnea symptoms. If your sleep apnea has worsened with weight gain or you have other medical conditions that warrant the use of GLP-1s, your healthcare provider may prescribe one, but they can be pricey. clusters.title: Scientists just found a hidden “drain” inside the human brain
clusters.summary: A hidden waste-removal pathway in the brain has finally been caught in action. Using cutting-edge MRI scans, researchers discovered that fluid flows along the middle meningeal artery in a slow, lymphatic-like pattern—very different from blood. This confirms the presence of a previously unknown drainage hub in humans.

items.title: Scientists just found a hidden “drain” inside the human brain
items.description: A hidden waste-removal pathway in the brain has finally been caught in action. Using cutting-edge MRI scans, researchers discovered that fluid flows along the middle meningeal artery in a slow, lymphatic-like pattern—very different from blood. This confirms the presence of a previously unknown drainage hub in humans. The finding could transform how scientists approach brain aging, injury, and diseases like Alzheimer’s.

clusters.title: Early weight gain is linked to lifelong health consequences
clusters.summary: Putting on weight earlier in life may be more dangerous than previously thought. Researchers found that early adulthood obesity significantly raises the risk of premature death, especially from major diseases like heart disease and diabetes. The longer the body carries excess weight, the greater the damage appears to be.

items.title: Early weight gain is linked to lifelong health consequences
items.description: Putting on weight earlier in life may be more dangerous than previously thought. Researchers found that early adulthood obesity significantly raises the risk of premature death, especially from major diseases like heart disease and diabetes. The longer the body carries excess weight, the greater the damage appears to be. Interestingly, cancer risk in women didn’t follow this pattern, suggesting other biological factors are at play.

clusters.title: As RSV evolves, a two‑pronged antibody cocktail aims to stay ahead
clusters.summary: Scientists in China have developed a two-antibody cocktail to treat respiratory syncytial virus, or RSV, that in laboratory studies prevented the virus from developing drug resistance—a persistent problem with current therapies for infants. RSV is a seasonal infection that circulates globally and can be especially dangerous for people at opposite ends of the age spectrum: infants and older adults. Vaccines are available for those who are 65 and older (GlaxoSmithKline's Arexvy, Pfizer's ABRYSVO and Moderna's mRESVIA), but there is still no available vaccine for infants who are among the most vulnerable.

items.title: As RSV evolves, a two‑pronged antibody cocktail aims to stay ahead
items.description: Scientists in China have developed a two-antibody cocktail to treat respiratory syncytial virus, or RSV, that in laboratory studies prevented the virus from developing drug resistance—a persistent problem with current therapies for infants. RSV is a seasonal infection that circulates globally and can be especially dangerous for people at opposite ends of the age spectrum: infants and older adults. Vaccines are available for those who are 65 and older (GlaxoSmithKline's Arexvy, Pfizer's ABRYSVO and Moderna's mRESVIA), but there is still no available vaccine for infants who are among the most vulnerable.

clusters.title: Scientists finally uncover why promising cancer drugs keep failing
clusters.summary: Cancer drugs known as BET inhibitors once looked like a breakthrough, but in real patients they’ve often fallen short. New research reveals a key reason why: two closely related proteins, BRD2 and BRD4, don’t actually do the same job. Instead, BRD2 acts like a “stage manager,” preparing genes for activation, while BRD4 triggers the final step that turns them on.

items.title: Scientists finally uncover why promising cancer drugs keep failing
items.description: Cancer drugs known as BET inhibitors once looked like a breakthrough, but in real patients they’ve often fallen short. New research reveals a key reason why: two closely related proteins, BRD2 and BRD4, don’t actually do the same job. Instead, BRD2 acts like a “stage manager,” preparing genes for activation, while BRD4 triggers the final step that turns them on. By blocking both at once, current drugs may be disrupting the process in unpredictable ways.

clusters.title: Extreme athletes just helped scientists unlock a deep evolutionary secret about human survival
clusters.summary: How does the human body manage its energy when pushed to the brink? New research shows that during extreme physical stress, deep evolutionary programming kicks in, sacrificing tissue repair and reproduction to keep the immune system active.

items.title: Extreme athletes just helped scientists unlock a deep evolutionary secret about human survival

clusters.title: Cancer cases in Canada projected to hit 254,100 in 2026
clusters.summary: Cancer rates in Canada will remain high in 2026. Lung, breast, prostate, and colorectal cancers will be the most common, making up 47% of new diagnoses, according to research published in CMAJ (Canadian Medical Association Journal).

items.title: Cancer cases in Canada projected to hit 254,100 in 2026
items.description: Cancer rates in Canada will remain high in 2026. Lung, breast, prostate, and colorectal cancers will be the most common, making up 47% of new diagnoses, according to research published in CMAJ (Canadian Medical Association Journal).

clusters.title: Your breathing pattern is as unique as a fingerprint
clusters.summary: By tracking continuous nasal airflow over 24 hours, researchers discovered that human breathing acts as a unique biometric fingerprint. This respiratory signature can identify individuals with near-perfect accuracy while predicting traits like mood, anxiety, and body mass.

items.title: Your breathing pattern is as unique as a fingerprint
items.description: By tracking continuous nasal airflow over 24 hours, researchers discovered that human breathing acts as a unique biometric fingerprint. This respiratory signature can identify individuals with near-perfect accuracy while predicting traits like mood, anxiety, and body mass.

clusters.title: Dragonflies can see a color humans can’t and it could change medicine
clusters.summary: Dragonflies may see the world in a way that pushes beyond human limits—and surprisingly, they do it using the same molecular trick we evolved ourselves. Scientists discovered that these insects can detect extremely deep red light, even edging into near-infrared, thanks to a specialized visual protein strikingly similar to the one in human eyes. This ability likely helps them spot mates mid-flight by picking up subtle differences in reflected light.

items.title: Dragonflies can see a color humans can’t and it could change medicine
items.description: Dragonflies may see the world in a way that pushes beyond human limits—and surprisingly, they do it using the same molecular trick we evolved ourselves. Scientists discovered that these insects can detect extremely deep red light, even edging into near-infrared, thanks to a specialized visual protein strikingly similar to the one in human eyes. This ability likely helps them spot mates mid-flight by picking up subtle differences in reflected light.

clusters.title: Scientists just watched Alzheimer’s damage happen in real time
clusters.summary: Scientists at Oregon State University have captured something researchers have long struggled to see: the real-time chemical interactions that help drive Alzheimer’s disease. By watching how metal ions—especially copper—trigger harmful protein clumping in the brain, the team uncovered a clearer picture of how the disease develops at a molecular level.

items.title: Scientists just watched Alzheimer’s damage happen in real time
items.description: Scientists at Oregon State University have captured something researchers have long struggled to see: the real-time chemical interactions that help drive Alzheimer’s disease. By watching how metal ions—especially copper—trigger harmful protein clumping in the brain, the team uncovered a clearer picture of how the disease develops at a molecular level.

clusters.title: Scientists finally crack mystery of rare COVID vaccine blood clots
clusters.summary: Researchers have uncovered why a rare blood clotting disorder can occur after certain COVID-19 vaccines or adenovirus infections. The immune system can mistakenly target a normal blood protein (PF4) after confusing it with a viral protein. This triggers clotting in extremely rare cases.

items.title: Scientists finally crack mystery of rare COVID vaccine blood clots
items.description: Researchers have uncovered why a rare blood clotting disorder can occur after certain COVID-19 vaccines or adenovirus infections. The immune system can mistakenly target a normal blood protein (PF4) after confusing it with a viral protein. This triggers clotting in extremely rare cases. The breakthrough means vaccines can now be redesigned to avoid this reaction while staying effective.

clusters.title: Scientists discover Alzheimer’s hidden “death switch” in the brain
clusters.summary: Scientists have uncovered a hidden “death switch” in the brain that may be driving Alzheimer’s disease—and even found a way to turn it off in mice. The culprit is a toxic pairing of two proteins that, when combined, triggers the destruction of brain cells and fuels memory loss. By using a new compound to break apart this deadly duo, researchers were able to slow disease progression, protect brain cells, and even reduce hallmark amyloid buildup.

items.title: Scientists discover Alzheimer’s hidden “death switch” in the brain

By using a new compound to break apart this deadly duo, researchers were able to slow disease progression, protect brain cells, and even reduce hallmark amyloid buildup.

clusters.title: Drumming with friends increases oxytocin levels in children, study finds
clusters.summary: A new Japanese study reveals that participating in a drum circle boosts oxytocin—the "bonding hormone"—in elementary school girls. However, this biological boost only happens when the children play alongside their friends, not strangers.

items.title: Drumming with friends increases oxytocin levels in children, study finds
items.description: A new Japanese study reveals that participating in a drum circle boosts oxytocin—the "bonding hormone"—in elementary school girls. However, this biological boost only happens when the children play alongside their friends, not strangers.

clusters.title: The hidden tradeoff behind today’s most popular weight loss drugs
clusters.summary: Weight loss drugs and bariatric surgery may work differently, but they lead to surprisingly similar results inside the body. Both significantly reduce fat while also causing a modest loss of muscle, reshaping overall body composition. Since muscle helps protect against early death, this balance matters more than the number on the scale.

items.title: The hidden tradeoff behind today’s most popular weight loss drugs
items.description: Weight loss drugs and bariatric surgery may work differently, but they lead to surprisingly similar results inside the body. Both significantly reduce fat while also causing a modest loss of muscle, reshaping overall body composition. Since muscle helps protect against early death, this balance matters more than the number on the scale. The study suggests these treatments improve health—but not without trade-offs.

clusters.title: States Face Another Challenge With Medicaid Work Rules: Staffing Shortages
clusters.summary: Some states already don’t have enough staff to quickly process Medicaid applications and answer enrollees’ phone calls. Researchers say they may not be prepared to handle new Medicaid work rules, predicting people will lose coverage as a result.
